reason: Closed-book solvability failure: at least one option labeled Correct (E) depends on paper-specific knowledge that the inter-particle redistribution/particle-by-particle lithiation effect is suppressed at high discharge rates. The stem provides only a very-low-rate (C/20) scenario and does not state any rate-dependence, so a test-taker without paper access cannot justify E from the scenario plus general reasoning. This makes the MCQ inadmissible in a closed-book setting.

MCQ with papers provided. The stem does not define the 'constant that governs the boundary temperature driving propagation' (constant C) or any boundary-temperature equation, yet option G is labeled Correct and depends on that paper-specific construct; this makes the item not closed-book solvable from the stem plus general engineering reasoning.

Closed-book solvability failure: option A is labeled Correct but depends on paper-specific separator melting point values and separator-type context (PE ~130°C vs PP ~170°C) to justify “most likely initiated” and “higher-melting separator type.” Those specifics are not provided in the stem, so an examinee without the papers cannot uniquely determine A as correct. This creates a hidden dependency on the literature rather than derivation from the stem plus general reasoning.

Short-answer item; papers provided. The stem supplies all needed assumptions (adiabatic low-rate cycling, SEI decomposition onset >60°C, regeneration following decomposition, observed capacity/voltage/heat-rate changes). The gold answer’s mechanism (regeneration consumes cyclable Li → capacity/voltage drop → higher overpotentials/transport losses → higher heat generation → temperature rise under adiabatic conditions → faster Arrhenius SEI decomposition → more exposed anode and regeneration) is logically derivable from the stem plus general electrochemical/thermal reasoning and is directly non-contradicted by the evidence (including explicit claims about >60°C Arrhenius SEI decomposition, decomposition exposing anode enabling exothermic regeneration, and regeneration causing capacity/voltage loss and increased heat generation). The statement that cathode inter-particle redistribution is not necessary once the anode-side regeneration loop is active is non-contradicted and consistent with evidence that cathode redistribution primarily affects initiation timing/heat generation at low rates. No fatal ambiguity or hidden paper-only dependency is required to answer.
